claimed_contribution: The paper defines and theorizes "retributive philanthropy" as charitable acts undertaken to punish a perceived volitional wrongdoer.
It provides multimethod evidence that this donation mode is emotionally, motivationally, and behaviorally distinct from traditional prosocial giving centered on helping motives.

It identifies practical levers for charitable marketers - perceived volitional wrongdoing, punishment efficacy, and donor authoritarianism - and argues retributive appeals can attract new donors and increase donations in suitable contexts.
context: The paper examines charitable giving episodes where donors use donations to punish perceived wrongdoers, such as donations in targets' names and protest-linked campaigns. Empirical settings span polarized public issues including the Canadian Freedom Convoy, antisemitism, racism, and war-related fundraising.
design_and_data: The research program includes 12 studies (7 primary and 5 supplementary) using qualitative, observational, and experimental methods. Study 1 conducts 10 semi-structured interviews with self-identified retributive donors and targets recruited via TikTok and email. Study 2 analyzes over 100,000 real donations and comments from a GiveSendGo campaign linked to the Canadian trucker protest movement, comparing comments that referenced GoFundMe with those that did not. Studies 3-7 are randomized experiments (student and Prolific samples) manipulating volitional vs nonvolitional wrongdoing, retributive option presence, ideological norm violations, and punishment efficacy, with outcomes including donation choice, donation likelihood, and donation amount, plus mediation and moderation tests.
main_findings: Interview and field evidence indicate that retributive donations are associated with perceived volitional wrongdoing, moral outrage, and punitive intent, and in the Freedom Convoy data donors mentioning GoFundMe gave more on average than those who did not.

Across experiments, framing wrongdoing as volitional (vs nonvolitional) increases retributive donation choice/likelihood, and this effect is transmitted by negative moral judgments and desire to punish, with other-condemning emotions (anger, contempt, disgust) also mediating.

Authoritarianism and punishment efficacy strengthen retributive giving: participants higher in ideologically congruent authoritarianism show greater willingness to make retributive donations, and high efficacy at punishing wrongdoers increases donation likelihood conditional on negative moral judgments.
one_line_summary: The paper shows that consumers sometimes donate to punish perceived volitional wrongdoers, and that this retributive philanthropy is driven by moral condemnation and punishment motives and amplified by authoritarianism and punishment efficacy.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prosocial behavior theory beyond altruistic and self-interested accounts by showing that retribution can motivate charitable giving through volitional wrongdoing, negative moral judgments, and punishment motives. It also refines efficacy-based donation research by distinguishing efficacy at punishing wrongdoers from efficacy at helping beneficiaries.
research_gap: Prosocial behavior research has largely focused on altruistic or self-interested donation motives and positive emotions, while high-profile punitive donation behaviors have not been systematically theorized or tested. The paper identifies a lack of empirical work on whether donations can function as retribution and on the mechanisms and boundary conditions of that process.
research_question: Can charitable giving operate as retribution, defined as punishing a perceived volitional wrongdoer, rather than only as altruism or self-interest? Which antecedents, mechanisms, and moderators - including volitional wrongdoing, negative moral judgments, desire to punish, other-condemning emotions, authoritarianism, and punishment efficacy - explain when consumers make retributive donations?
